SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The paper states clear main claims and presents extensive experiments (400 synthetic benchmarks, three gene-expression sets, 1000 Genomes) and comparative baselines, but experimental details and statistical reporting are incomplete (no p-values, no multiple-comparison correction, limited description of parameter tuning and randomness). The mathematical model and constrained posterior (Eq. 4) are plausible and referenced to prior work, but the manuscript asserts strong guarantees (e.g. that a single projected-Newton step in the E-step “still guarantees the correct solution”) without a proof or sufficient justification in the text. Overall the contribution appears technically promising and partially supported, but there are gaps in analysis and in the reporting of experiments and theoretical claims that prevent a higher technical score.

CLARITY.justification: The paper is generally well-organized (Intro → Methods with clear Eqns (1)–(4) → Experiments → Conclusion) and states the main contribution (RFNs address FABIA shortcomings) early in the Introduction. Notation is mostly defined in the Methods (e.g. v, h, W, ⌃p) but a few symbols/constraints are presented tersely and could use clearer explanation. Figures/tables are referenced (Fig.1, Table1/2, Fig.2) and the synthetic-data procedures are described in detail, but the actual figures/tables are not present in the text provided which weakens assessment of their effectiveness. Readability is adequate overall but marred by repeated/garbled paragraphs and formatting artifacts near the end (many duplicated citation lines and concatenated sentences), which reduces clarity and suggests proofreading is needed.

ORIGINALITY.justification: The core RFN machinery (rectified-posteriors, posterior regularization, projected Newton E‑step) is presented as prior work (Clevert et al., 2015), so the paper does not introduce a fundamentally new modeling paradigm. Its novelty is an application/extension of RFNs to biclustering, adding a Laplace prior on weights and dropout on code units to promote feature sparsity and decorrelation, and demonstrating empirical gains over FABIA and other methods. Biclustering itself is a well‑studied problem, and the technical changes are sensible but incremental (combining known ingredients: rectification, Laplace sparsity, dropout, posterior regularization) rather than a radical new technique, so the contribution is moderately novel and of practical interest to experts.
